section_path: Natural Diamond Council response to the public consultation on Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Natural Diamond Council (NDC) is a not-for-profit member association with the mission to advance the integrity of the modern diamond jewellery industry and inspire, educate, and protect the consumer. With offices in Paris, London, New York, Mumbai and Shanghai, the NDC supports the diamond jewellery industry by providing transparency and insight on the progress of this sector and its commitments to further betterment.

section_path: Natural Diamond Council response to the public consultation on Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The Natural Diamond Council welcomes the opportunity to contribute to the fitness check (evaluation) on digital fairness identified gaps in consumer protection online. Ensuring EU consumers can buy confidently, make informed purchasing decisions and ensuring fair commercial practices is paramount.

section_path: Natural Diamond Council response to the public consultation on Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: We would like to flag misrepresentation and misleading online marketing (including influencers and social media) of synthetic diamonds which have a considerate impact on EU consumer protection and their trust in the European diamond jewellery sector, as well as the strength of the EU economy. These unfair commercial practices (encountered across all communication mediums) are done through misleading terminology usage, vague marketing as well as inconsistent disclosure. According to international industry guidelines and standards, synthetic diamond manufacturers and sellers should disclose the type of their stones at all times, and use the following specific terminology that best reflects the nature of the product (this terminology leaves space for national application where language differs): "synthetic diamonds", "laboratory-grown diamonds" or "laboratory-created diamonds" (ISO standard 18323:2015).

section_path: Natural Diamond Council response to the public consultation on Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: The Natural Diamond Council therefore respectfully asks for strengthened measures to protect EU consumers online while ensuring the growth of European diamond jewellery sector by:

section_path: Natural Diamond Council response to the public consultation on Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Requiring appropriate disclosure of synthetic diamonds to prevent fraud and false claims in online retail markets and platforms, also including influencer marketing

section_path: Natural Diamond Council response to the public consultation on Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Implementing harmonised terminology across the EU Single Market in order to help consumers make informed choices

section_path: Natural Diamond Council response to the public consultation on Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: Natural Diamond Council - Milton Gate, 60 Chiswell Street, London EC1Y 4AG www.naturaldiamonds.com

section_path: Introduction to natural diamonds and synthetic diamonds
paragraphs.text: Natural and synthetic diamonds are two separate products, with different value propositions, that can easily be separated with Diamond Verification Instruments. Both products have an equal right to be marketed to consumers. The issue resides in lack of proper continuous disclosure and misleading marketing of synthetic diamonds. Consumers risk economic harm if they were to purchase a 'synthetic diamond' thinking it is a 'natural diamond'. Lack of disclosure online, on websites, in social media campaigns, by influencers, also creates an unfair trading environment for companies.

section_path: Introduction to natural diamonds and synthetic diamonds
paragraphs.text: The main concern we have is how each company repeatedly refers to their laboratory-grown diamond product in consumer communications as "diamonds" without qualification and the customer journey to making a purchase does not accurately disclose the nature of the product.

section_path: Introduction to natural diamonds and synthetic diamonds
paragraphs.text: Natural diamonds are gemstones that were formed one to three billion years ago. They originated about 100 miles beneath the earth's surface, created up to 3.5 billion years ago and were propelled to the surface by volcanic eruptions that occurred between 50 million and 1.6 billion years ago. They are found in some of the most remote places on earth and require significant investment and technical expertise to locate and recover.

section_path: Introduction to natural diamonds and synthetic diamonds
paragraphs.text: The supply of large stones is particularly rare; it is estimated that, today, the global annual recovery of diamonds larger than 1 carat would fill one exercise ball, while the annual recovery of all diamonds with a weight of 5+ carats is less than a basketball. It is this combination of rarity, authenticity, and preciousness that drives significant demand for and value in natural diamonds.

section_path: Introduction to natural diamonds and synthetic diamonds
paragraphs.text: While synthetic diamonds (also referred to as laboratory-created diamonds and laboratory-grown diamonds) have essentially the same physical and chemical characteristics as natural diamonds, but their differences can be reliably detected by available equipment. Laboratory-grown diamonds are mass-produced manufactured stones, created in two to three weeks using either of two methods: High Pressure High Temperature ("HPHT") or Chemical Vapor Deposition ("CVD"). Each method leaves growth marks and tell-tale signs that are distinctive of an artificially produced stone. Around 60% of global synthetic diamond production comes from China and India. The production process of synthetic diamonds was initiated by ASEA and General Electrics in the 1950s. By the 1970s the production process was developed to deliver synthetic diamonds more visually like natural diamonds. Due to the costly energy

section_path: Introduction to natural diamonds and synthetic diamonds
paragraphs.text: intense production process, it took another 30 years before synthetic diamonds were offered as a consumer product. The proliferation of synthetic diamonds in the jewellery industry gained momentum in the past couple of years in tandem with swiftly decreasing retail prices.

section_path: Unfair commercial practices and misleading online practices
paragraphs.text: Both natural diamonds and synthetic diamonds have equal right to be marketed as long as consumers are well informed of the nature and presentation of the product they are purchasing.

section_path: Unfair commercial practices and misleading online practices
paragraphs.text: The concern is with the consumer confusion and deception that occurs when synthetic diamonds are not properly disclosed as such especially in the rapidly growing e-commerce / online market where consumers rely solely on what is communicated through a website or social media post. They are often just called "diamonds", "lab-diamonds" or even use other conspicuous terms such as "sustainably made diamonds" or "ethically created diamonds".

section_path: Unfair commercial practices and misleading online practices
paragraphs.text: The use of hashtags sometimes masks and replaces proper disclosure in more prominent ways. An image of a diamond piece of jewellery is often used without clear disclosure of the type of stone, with the consumers being suggested through the image that this could actually be a natural diamond instead of a synthetic one.

section_path: Unfair commercial practices and misleading online practices
paragraphs.text: Influencers also speak vaguely about "diamonds" and, if at all disclosing synthetic diamonds, they do so in hashtags accompanying their posts, rather than in the clips themselves or live feeds.

section_path: Unfair commercial practices and misleading online practices
paragraphs.text: On marketing practices and communication channels, whilst there are national advertising and marketing codes, as well as request for disclosure under the EU Unfair Commercial Practices Directive – these are not sufficient, leave space for interpretation, and lack enforcement. This results in companies not including the nature of their stones in key sections of their websites and leaving them ambiguous even at the point of sale.

section_path: Unfair commercial practices and misleading online practices
paragraphs.text: The potential for consumer harm is significant, as they are misled into the product they are purchasing and particularly as natural diamonds and synthetic diamonds have fundamentally different price structures/trajectories.

section_path: Unfair commercial practices and misleading online practices
paragraphs.text: Diamond analyst Paul Zimnisky has tracked trends in laboratory-grown diamonds and natural diamonds since 2015 for various size bands. His analysis suggests that laboratory-grown diamonds now cost around 20% of the price of a comparable natural diamond (i.e they are 80% cheaper) for sizes of 1 carat and over. The production costs for laboratory-grown diamonds have fallen by 90 percent over the last decade due to the development of CVD technology; economies of scale

section_path: Unfair commercial practices and misleading online practices
paragraphs.text: Natural Diamond Council - Milton Gate, 60 Chiswell Street, London EC1Y 4AG www.naturaldiamonds.com

section_path: Unfair commercial practices and misleading online practices
paragraphs.text: as current producers expand; new producers entering the market; and the repurposing of thousands of HPHT machines in China from producing industrial grade synthetic diamond abrasives marketed for industrial usage to "jewellery-quality" synthetic diamonds aimed toward consumers. Production costs are likely to continue to decrease through further technological advances and competitive forces as supply increases.

section_path: Unfair commercial practices and misleading online practices
paragraphs.text: Prices of laboratory-grown diamonds continue to decline due to mass production with falling production cost. For example, over the last ten years, the retail price of a 1.5 carat laboratory-grown stone has fallen 86% from approximately €10,190 ($10,750) to €1,370 ($1,455).

section_path: A fragmented picture of legislation and industry self-regulation
paragraphs.text: There is a clear lack of an enforceable legal framework with standardised agreed language that protects consumers with regards to disclosure and harmonised clear terminology.

section_path: A fragmented picture of legislation and industry self-regulation
paragraphs.text: The difference between natural diamonds and synthetic diamonds was acknowledged by the EU institutions through the Guidance Notice (2021/C 526/01) on the interpretation and application of Directive 2005/29/EC of the European Parliament and of the Council concerning unfair business-to-consumer commercial practices in the internal market. It notes that "more complex goods may require the communication of additional information – on the product description tags in the shop or on online pages – to establish their main characteristics. [...] Such characteristics could be, for example, the limitation of the duration or of the nature and performance of a service (e.g. whether a 'fibre' internet service is 'fibre-to-the-home' or another type) or a particular composition or specification of goods (e.g. the synthetic origin of precious stones such as diamonds)".

section_path: A fragmented picture of legislation and industry self-regulation
paragraphs.text: There are international sectorial guidelines with regards to both suggested terminology as well as obligations of disclosure, developed under the World Jewellery Confederation (CIBJO) and signed by the leading jewellery industry organisations, including in Europe. The ISO standard 18323:2015 provides a series of descriptors to differentiate natural from synthetic diamonds (also called "laboratory-grown diamonds or laboratory-created diamonds) and obliges the issuance of certificates to consumers. The ISO standard 18323:2015 has also been adopted by the European Committee for Standardization CEN/TC 410. The added value of

section_path: A fragmented picture of legislation and industry self-regulation
paragraphs.text: these standards is limited by the fact that they have to be transposed into national law to become enforceable. These guidelines and standards require sellers of laboratory-grown diamonds to use, clearly and conspicuously, the below terminology:

section_path: A fragmented picture of legislation and industry self-regulation
paragraphs.text: When referring to synthetic diamonds:

section_path: A fragmented picture of legislation and industry self-regulation
paragraphs.text: Use one of the following authorised qualifiers when referring to synthetic diamonds: “synthetic”, “laboratory-grown” or “laboratory-created”.

section_path: A fragmented picture of legislation and industry self-regulation
paragraphs.text: Do not use abbreviations such as “lab-grown” and “lab-created”.

section_path: A fragmented picture of legislation and industry self-regulation
paragraphs.text: Do not use the following terms: “cultured diamonds” and “cultivated diamonds” as “cultured” and “cultivated” refer exclusively to organic/biogenic products.

section_path: A fragmented picture of legislation and industry self-regulation
paragraphs.text: Do not use the following terms: “real”, “genuine”, “precious”, “authentic” and “natural” as those apply exclusively to natural minerals and gemstones.

section_path: A fragmented picture of legislation and industry self-regulation
paragraphs.text: A diamond is natural by definition. Therefore, use the word “diamond” without a qualifier when referring to a diamond. If differentiation from synthetic diamonds is required, use the term “natural diamonds” as term of equivalent meaning.

section_path: A fragmented picture of legislation and industry self-regulation
paragraphs.text: In terms of legislation, there is no harmonised approach across the EU Single Market, which damages the protection of EU consumers as well as the European jewellery sector. France adopted a national legislation in 2002 (Decree n° 2002-65 of 14 January 2002) related to the trade of gemstones and pearls which requires the use of terminology of “synthetic” diamonds to all those that are not natural gemstones. The law forbids the use of several words such as “cultivated”, “real”, and “cultured” for synthetic diamonds. In Belgium there are legal requirements but only for business-to-business transactions. There is the Belgian “Royal Decree on the details of pre-contractual information provided to consumers on natural and synthetic diamonds”¹⁴. This legislation obliges jewellers to inform consumers on the characteristics of the diamonds which are incorporated in the jewel but only just before the sale papers are handed over to the consumer and does not apply to marketing activations pre-sale.

section_path: A fragmented picture of legislation and industry self-regulation
paragraphs.text: For the foregoing reasons, we respectfully ask that the European Commission consider protecting EU consumers and supporting the European diamond jewellery industry by
